{{Short description|American psychologist}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=June 2023}}
{{Infobox scientist
| name              = Dacher Keltner
| image             = Prof. Dacher Keltner.jpg
| image_size        = 220px
| alt               = Dacher Keltner, wearing a blue t-shirt by a Redwood tree, smiling at the camera
| caption           = Dacher Keltner in 2023
| birth_place       = [[Jalisco]], Mexico
| workplaces        = [[University of California, Berkeley]] 1996–present<br />[[University of Wisconsin–Madison]] 1992–1996
| alma_mater        = [[University of California at Santa Barbara]] ([[Bachelor of Arts|BA]])<br />[[Stanford University]] ([[PhD]])
| doctoral_advisor  = [[Lee Ross]]
| thesis_title      = Misperceptions of the Other Side's Views: A Source of Conflict and Conflict Resolution
| thesis_url        = http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/38687943
| thesis_year       = 1989
| doctoral_students = 
| website           = {{URL|http://psychology.berkeley.edu/people/dacher-keltner}}
| field             = [[Psychology]]
}}

'''Dacher Joseph Keltner''' is a Mexican-born American professor of [[psychology]] at the [[University of California, Berkeley]], who directs the Berkeley Social Interaction Lab.<ref>{{cite web|title=Berkeley Social Interaction Lab|url=https://bsil.berkeley.edu/|website=BSI Lab|access-date=October 29, 2019}}</ref>

==Early life and education ==
Keltner was born in [[Jalisco]], Mexico, to two early members of the counterculture. Keltner's mother, a literature professor, and father, an artist and firefighter, raised both him and his brother in [[Laurel Canyon]] in the 1970s. When his mother secured her first job as a professor, they moved to a conservative town in the foothills of the California Sierra Nevada. When Keltner was in high school, their family relocated to Nottingham, England.<ref>{{cite book |last=Cain |first=Susan |author-link=Susan Cain |date=2022 |title=Bittersweet: How Sorrow and Longing Make Us Whole |publisher=Crown |pages=7–8 |isbn=978-0-4514-9978-3 |chapter=What is sadness good for? |language=English}}</ref>

Keltner received his [[Bachelor of Arts|B.A.]] in psychology and [[sociology]] from the [[University of California, Santa Barbara]], in 1984, he received his [[Ph.D.]] from [[Stanford University]] in 1989, and he completed three years of post-doctoral work with [[Paul Ekman]] at the [[University of California, San Francisco]].<ref name=about>{{cite web|last1=Dacher|first1=Keltner|title=About|url=http://greatergood.berkeley.edu/dacherkeltner/about.php|publisher=The Greater Good Science Center|access-date=September 19, 2016}}</ref>

==Academic career==
Keltner began his academic career at the [[University of Wisconsin–Madison]], and then returned to  [[University of California, Berkeley]]'s Psychology Department in 1996 attaining full professorship in 2002.<ref name=vitae>{{cite web|last1=Dacher|first1=Keltner|title=Curriculum vitae|url=http://greatergood.berkeley.edu/dacherkeltner/docs/vita_dacherkeltner.pdf|publisher=Greater Good Science Center|access-date=September 19, 2016}}</ref>

His research focuses on the cultural and evolutionary origins of [[compassion]], [[awe]], [[love]], [[beauty]], and [[Power (social and political)|power]], [[social class]], and [[social inequality]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://psychology.berkeley.edu/people/dacher-keltner|title=Dacher Keltner - UC Psych|website=psychology.berkeley.edu}}</ref>

Keltner is the co-author of two textbooks, as well as the best-selling ''Born to Be Good: The Science of a Meaningful Life'', ''The Compassionate Instinct'', ''The Power Paradox: How We Gain and Lose Influence'', and most recently, the national bestseller ''AWE: The New Science of Everyday Wonder and How it Can Transform Your Life.'' Keltner has published over 230 scientific articles and has written for ''[[The New York Times Magazine]]'', ''[[The New York Times]]'', ''[[The London Times]]'', ''[[The Wall Street Journal]]'', [[SLATE]]<!-- Should this be [[SLATE]] or ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]''? -->, ''[[Utne Reader]]''. He has received numerous national prizes and grants for his research, teaching, and writing, and is a member of the american Academy of Arts and Sciences.<ref name=vitae/>

His Science of Happiness [[MOOC]] at [[EdX]] has had over 600,000 enrollees. ''[[Wired (magazine)|Wired]]'' magazine recently rated his podcasts from his course ''Emotion'' as one of the five best educational downloads, and the ''[[Utne Reader]]'' selected Keltner for one of its 50 2008 visionaries.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.utne.com/2008-11-13/50-visionaries-who-are-changing-your-world.aspx|title=50 Visionaries Who Are Changing Your World|access-date=April 21, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150423164521/http://www.utne.com/2008-11-13/50-visionaries-who-are-changing-your-world.aspx|archive-date=April 23, 2015|url-status=dead}}</ref>

Keltner has collaborated with directors at [[Pixar]], including film director and animator [[Pete Docter]] in his films ''[[Inside Out (2015 film)|Inside Out]]''<ref name="DKeltner2015">{{cite web|url=https://psmag.com/social-justice/a-conversation-with-psychologist-behind-inside-out|title=A Conversation With the Psychologist Behind 'Inside Out'|author=J. Wesley Judd|work=[[Pacific Standard]]|publisher=Miller-McCune Center for Research, Media and Public Policy|date=July 8, 2015|access-date=July 9, 2015}}</ref> and [[Soul (2020 film)|''Soul.'']]<ref>{{Cite web |title=How the Science of Awe Shaped Pixar's "Soul" |url=https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/article/item/how_the_science_of_awe_shaped_pixars_soul |access-date=2023-08-07 |website=Greater Good |language=en}}</ref> He has worked and continues to work with [[Facebook]] engineers and designers on projects such as Facebook stickers and [[Facebook reactions]].<ref>{{Cite magazine|url=https://www.wired.com/2016/02/facebook-reactions-totally-redesigned-like-button/|title=Facebook Reactions, the Totally Redesigned Like Button, Is Here|magazine='[[Wired (magazine)|Wired]] |access-date=September 19, 2017|language=en-US}}</ref> He has also worked on projects at Google on altruism and emotion, and was recently{{when|date=May 2022}} featured in [[Tom Shadyac]]’s movie ''I Am''.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.calprojectawe.org/team |title=Project Awe Team |access-date=April 21, 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150622113503/https://www.calprojectawe.org/team |archive-date=June 22, 2015 |url-status=dead }}</ref>

Keltner is collaborated with the [[Sierra Club]] to get veterans and inner city adolescents outdoors.  Building upon his experiences in a restorative justice program with prisoners in [[San Quentin Prison]], Keltner wrote a brief for a case – ''Ashker v. Governor of California'' – that led to the curtailment of solitary confinement in maximum-security prisons in California.

He is also the founder and faculty director of the [[Greater Good Science Center]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Staff |url=http://greatergood.berkeley.edu/about/people#staff |access-date=September 19, 2016 |website=Greater Good Science Center}}</ref>

===Theory of power===
Together with [[Deborah H. Gruenfeld]] of the [[Stanford Graduate School of Business]] and Cameron Anderson,<ref>{{cite web|title=Faculty and Executive Leadership Directory|url=http://facultybio.haas.berkeley.edu/faculty-list/anderson-cameron/|publisher=Haas School of Business|access-date=September 19, 2016}}</ref> psychologist at the [[Haas School of Business]] at UC Berkeley, Keltner has developed the [[Approach/Inhibition Theory of Power]], which aims to present an integrative account of the effects of power on human behavior, suggesting that the acquisition of power has a disinhibiting effect regarding the social consequences of exercising it.<ref name=WSJ>{{cite news|last1=Lehrer|first1=Jonah|author-link=Jonah Lehrer |title=The Power Trip|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB10001424052748704407804575425561952689390|work=[[The Wall Street Journal]]|date=August 14, 2010|quote= Contrary to the Machiavellian cliché, nice people are more likely to rise to power. Then something strange happens: Authority atrophies the very talents that got them there.}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=Robertson|first1=Ian H.|author-link=Ian Robertson (psychologist)|title=How power affects the brain|url=https://thepsychologist.bps.org.uk/volume-26/edition-3/how-power-affects-brain|publisher=[[British Psychological Society]]|date=March 2013}}</ref><ref name =KGA2003>{{cite journal|last1=Keltner|first1=Dacher|last2=Gruenfeld|first2=Deborah H|last3=Anderson|first3=Cameron|title=Power, Approach and Inhibition|journal=Psychological Review|url=http://ist-socrates.berkeley.edu/~keltner/publications/keltner.power.psychreview.2003.pdf|volume=110|issue=2|year=2003|pages=265–284|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.110.2.265|citeseerx=10.1.1.203.2748|access-date=December 3, 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141130122820/http://ist-socrates.berkeley.edu/~keltner/publications/keltner.power.psychreview.2003.pdf|archive-date=November 30, 2014|url-status=dead|pmid=12747524}}</ref>

===Social class===
With collaborators [[Paul Piff]] and Michael Kraus, Keltner has offered a theoretical account of how social class shapes human thought, feeling, and action.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kraus |first1=Michael W. |last2=Piff |first2=Paul K. |last3=Mendoza-Denton |first3=Rodolfo |last4=Rheinschmidt |first4=Michelle L. |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |date=2012 |title=Social class, solipsism, and contextualism: How the rich are different from the poor. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/a0028756 |journal=Psychological Review |language=en |volume=119 |issue=3 |pages=546–572 |doi=10.1037/a0028756 |pmid=22775498 |issn=1939-1471|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In empirical demonstrations of this work, Keltner has shown that people from more privileged class backgrounds are more likely to drive through pedestrian crosswalks and cheat on tests to win a prize, feel less compassion than those who suffer, and explain their success in terms of their own superior traits.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Stancato |first2=Daniel M. |last3=Côté |first3=Stéphane |last4=Mendoza-Denton |first4=Rodolfo |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |date=2012-03-13 |title=Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=109 |issue=11 |pages=4086–4091 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1118373109 |doi-access=free |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=3306667 |pmid=22371585|bibcode=2012PNAS..109.4086P }}</ref>

===Human emotion===
Keltner has been a central voice in making the case that emotions serve important social functions, enabling us to fold into relationships vital to survival, like friendships, groups, romantic partnerships, and parent-child attachments.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Keltner |first1=Dacher |last2=Sauter |first2=Disa |last3=Tracy |first3=Jessica L. |last4=Wetchler |first4=Everett |last5=Cowen |first5=Alan S. |date=2022-04-03 |title=How emotions, relationships, and culture constitute each other: advances in social functionalist theory |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02699931.2022.2047009 |journal=Cognition and Emotion |language=en |volume=36 |issue=3 |pages=388–401 |doi=10.1080/02699931.2022.2047009 |pmid=35639090 |issn=0269-9931|url-access=subscription |hdl=11245.1/1c025023-a664-4c92-b943-a8c9762d24ce |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Guided by this framework, Keltner has done pioneering work on emotions like embarrassment, shame, love, compassion, amusement, and gratitude.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Goetz |first1=Jennifer L. |last2=Keltner |first2=Dacher |last3=Simon-Thomas |first3=Emiliana |date=May 2010 |title=Compassion: An evolutionary analysis and empirical review. |journal=Psychological Bulletin |language=en |volume=136 |issue=3 |pages=351–374 |doi=10.1037/a0018807 |issn=1939-1455 |pmc=2864937 |pmid=20438142}}</ref>

Beginning with his post-doctoral fellowship with Paul Ekman, Keltner has long studied emotional expression from a Basic Emotion perspective.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Keltner |first1=Dacher |last2=Tracy |first2=Jessica L. |last3=Sauter |first3=Disa |last4=Cowen |first4=Alan |date=2019-06-01 |title=What Basic Emotion Theory Really Says for the Twenty-First Century Study of Emotion |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10919-019-00298-y |journal=Journal of Nonverbal Behavior |language=en |volume=43 |issue=2 |pages=195–201 |doi=10.1007/s10919-019-00298-y |issn=1573-3653 |pmc=6688640 |pmid=31404243}}</ref> He has done work documenting the universality of upwards of 20 distinct facial expressions, the richness with which people can communicate emotion in the voice, and how people communicate emotions like love, compassion, and gratitude through touch. In partnership with Alan Cowen, he has offered a new computational perspective on what emotions are.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cowen |first1=Alan S. |last2=Keltner |first2=Dacher |date=2017-09-19 |title=Self-report captures 27 distinct categories of emotion bridged by continuous gradients |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=114 |issue=38 |pages=E7900–E7909 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1702247114 |doi-access=free |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=5617253 |pmid=28874542|bibcode=2017PNAS..114E7900C }}</ref> With a “data-driven” approach that maps people’s emotional experiences and expressions across the widest array of emotions studied to date and across different cultures, this work is finding that the emotion space involves upwards of 20 distinct states, that blends in emotion are common, that each emotion category has many variations within it, and that discrete emotion concepts (e.g., “awe” “sympathy”) rather than broader constructs such as valence or arousal drive the representation of emotional experience and recognition of emotion.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cowen |first1=Alan |last2=Sauter |first2=Disa |last3=Tracy |first3=Jessica L. |last4=Keltner |first4=Dacher |date=July 2019 |title=Mapping the Passions: Toward a High-Dimensional Taxonomy of Emotional Experience and Expression |journal=Psychological Science in the Public Interest |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=69–90 |doi=10.1177/1529100619850176 |issn=1529-1006 |pmc=6675572 |pmid=31313637}}</ref>

===Awe===
In 2003, Keltner and collaborator Jonathan Haidt authored a paper charting what awe is and how it influences our moral, spiritual and aesthetic lives.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Keltner |first1=Dacher |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |date=January 2003 |title=Approaching awe, a moral, spiritual, and aesthetic emotion |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02699930302297 |journal=Cognition and Emotion |language=en |volume=17 |issue=2 |pages=297–314 |doi=10.1080/02699930302297 |pmid=29715721 |issn=0269-9931|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Building upon that paper, Keltner has done over 15 years of science on awe, summarized in ''AWE: The New Science of Everyday Wonder and how it Can Transform Your Life''. This research shows that people find experiences of awe in what he calls the eight wonders of life: the moral beauty of others, nature, moving in unison, music, visual art, spirituality, big ideas, and life and death. Awe enables individuals to integrate into strong communities by inspiring cooperative tendencies and a more collective self. In a recent paper with collaborator Maria Monroy, Keltner has made the case that experiences of awe account for why things like music, spirituality, and psychedelics benefit health and well-being.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Monroy |first1=Maria |last2=Keltner |first2=Dacher |date=March 2023 |title=Awe as a Pathway to Mental and Physical Health |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=309–320 |doi=10.1177/17456916221094856 |issn=1745-6916 |pmc=10018061 |pmid=35994778}}</ref>

===Science of happiness===
In his book ''Born to be Good: The Science of a Meaningful Life'', Keltner explores the science behind well-being. The book attempts to counter the bias that we are wired to be self-interested. Keltner explores the [[Confucian]] idea of the ''jen'' ratio; the relationship between actions that bring the good of others to completion and those that bring out bad. The greater score is a direct relation to your happiness. In the book he touches on the qualities of gratitude, compassion, play, awe, embarrassment and teasing and how these qualities are innate in people but also can be developed.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Grothe|first1=D.J.|author-link=D.J. Grothe|title=Dacher Keltner - Born to be Good|url=http://www.pointofinquiry.org/dacher_keltner_born_to_be_good/|website=Point of Inquiry|date=April 3, 2009 |publisher=Center for Inquiry|access-date=September 19, 2016}}</ref>

==Personal life==
Keltner lives in [[Berkeley, California]].<ref name=about/> He served as a consultant for the film [[Inside Out 2]] and he is the host of the podcast ''[[The Science of Happiness]]''.<ref>{{cite web |title=science-happiness |url=https://www.pri.org/programs/science-happiness |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180129174818/https://www.pri.org/programs/science-happiness |url-status=dead |archive-date=January 29, 2018 |access-date=May 21, 2019 |website=Greater Good Science Center}}</ref>

W. Norton & Co. |isbn=9780393337280 |oclc=317920038}}
* {{cite book |ref=wcPP |title=The Power Paradox: How We Gain and Lose Influence |first=Dacher |last=Keltner |date=2016 |place=New York |publisher=Penguin Press |isbn=9781594205248 |oclc=929055993}}
* {{cite book |ref=wcUE |title=Understanding Emotions |first1=Dacher |last1=Keltner |first2=Keith |last2=Oatley |first3=Jennifer M |last3=Jenkins |edition=4th |date=2019 |isbn=9781119492535 |oclc=1045639702}} prev. ed. published by Blackwell Publishers, 1996.
* {{cite book |ref=wcA |last=Keltner |first=Dacher |date=January 3, 2023 |title=Awe: The New Science of Everyday Wonder and How It Can Transform Your Life |publisher=Penguin Press |pages=336 |isbn=9781984879684| oclc=1345466567}}
* {{cite book |ref=wcSP |title=Social Psychology |first1=Gilovich |last1=Thomas |first2=Dacher |last2=Keltner| first3=Serena| last3=Chen| first4=Richard E |last4=Nisbett |edition=6th |date=2023 |place=New York |publisher=W. W. Norton & Co. |isbn=9781324045557 |oclc=}} prev. ed. published by W. W. Norton & Co., 2005.
